basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: Al-Furqān — “the Criterion,” that which separates true from false — opens on the blessing (tabāraka) of Him who sent down this Book upon His servant, that he might be a warner to the worlds. Qushayrī makes of the word tabāraka the confluence of all praise: praise by the essence, by the attribute, by the bounty. The sūra confronts the deniers' objection — what sort of messenger is this, who eats and walks in the markets? — and answers it with the dignity of servitude: when offered the keys of the world's treasures, the Prophet preferred to be “a patient, thankful servant.” Then unfold the great cosmic signs, in which Qushayrī reads the states of the soul: how He extends the shadow of His solicitude then draws it in toward Himself, making His servants alternate between extinction and subsistence; the night-garment and sleep-repose, where the heedless sleep while the lovers keep vigil; the winds heralding His mercy, which are also the breaths of sanctity that purify hearts of all self-will; the two seas, sweet and bitter, image of hearts fashioned now as a mine of certainty, now as a vessel of doubt; and the constellations of the sky, doubled by the stars of the heart — intelligence, the gnosis whose moon never wanes, the unification whose sun never sets. The sūra culminates on the portrait of the servants of the All-Merciful (ʿibād al-Raḥmān): they walk the earth in humility, answer “Peace” to the ignorant, pass the night prostrate and standing, keep the just measure in spending, call upon no other god and bear no false witness — and at the close, it is supplication (duʿāʾ) that gives man all his worth before God.
themes: the Criterion sent down upon the servant
the deniers' objection and the dignity of servitude
the cosmic signs and the states of the heart
the shadow, the night, the winds, the two seas
repentance and the worth of supplication

versets.ar: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَالَمِينَ نَذِيرًا
versets.tr: tabāraka lladhī nazzala l-furqāna ʿalā ʿabdihi li-yakūna li-l-ʿālamīna nadhīran
versets.trad: Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His servant, that he might be a warner to the worlds,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: tabāraka, of the form tafāʿala, denotes the permanence of His subsistence, the eternity of His being that knows neither beginning nor end. The exegetes read it as “He has exalted and magnified Himself”; others hear in it baraka, the overflow of grace and benefit. Thus all praise gathers into three modes: praise by His essence and His right, praise by His attribute and His glory, praise by His bounty and His favor — and the word tabāraka draws them all together. He sent down the Furqān upon His servant: He honored him by making him a prophet, preferred him, sent him to creation, and made this Qurʾān the sign of his truthfulness, a luminous lamp.

mots.def: form tafāʿala: permanence of being and overflow of grace.
that which separates true from false; a name of the Qurʾān.
ʿabd: servant; title of honor of the Prophet.
nadhīr: one who warns of punishment.

versets.ar: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيرًا
versets.tr: alladhī lahu mulku s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-lam yattakhidh waladan wa-lam yakun lahu sharīkun fi l-mulki wa-khalaqa kulla shayʾin fa-qaddarahu taqdīran
versets.trad: He to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, who has taken no child, who has no partner in dominion, and who created everything, ordaining it in a measure.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He is alone in kingship, no partner shares it with Him; alone in majesty, no peer measures it with Him. He is the One with none to divide Him in His essence, no partner in His creatures, no likeness in His right or His attributes. Everything He created, then assigned it its precise measure: nothing escapes His decree, and every existent bears the exact mark His wisdom has set upon it.

versets.ar: وَاتَّخَذُوا مِن دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لَّا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئًا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَاةً وَلَا نُشُورًا
versets.tr: wa-ttakhadhū min dūnihi ālihatan lā yakhluqūna shayʾan wa-hum yukhlaqūna wa-lā yamlikūna li-anfusihim ḍarran wa-lā nafʿan wa-lā yamlikūna mawtan wa-lā ḥayātan wa-lā nushūran
versets.trad: Yet they have taken, besides Him, gods who create nothing and are themselves created; who have no power over harm or benefit to themselves, and no power over death, or life, or resurrection.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they took besides God gods that possess not even the husk of a date-stone, create not the hollow of a pit, ward off neither great harm nor small, are of no benefit to them and ease no hardship; they have no power over anyone's death, nor over resurrection. The whole argument lies in this contrast: one who has no power over himself — how could he be a god for another?

mots.racine: ض ر ر / ن ف ع

mots.def: nushūr: the unfolding, raising of the dead.

versets.ar: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هَٰذَا إِلَّا إِفْكٌ افْتَرَاهُ وَأَعَانَهُ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ آخَرُونَ ۖ فَقَدْ جَاءُوا ظُلْمًا وَزُورًا
versets.tr: wa-qāla lladhīna kafarū in hādhā illā ifkun iftarāhu wa-aʿānahu ʿalayhi qawmun ākharūna fa-qad jāʾū ẓulman wa-zūran
versets.trad: The disbelievers say: “This is nothing but a fabrication he has forged, and other folk have helped him with it.” Thus they have come with wrong and falsehood.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they judged by what they themselves were; since they were wont to call on their own kind for aid in what was beyond them, they imagined the Prophet did the same. But their word is without proof: they spoke with no argument, and what they call fabrication is only their own incapacity projected onto the inimitable.

mots.racine: ظ ل م / ز و ر

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَهَا فَهِيَ تُمْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-qālū asāṭīru l-awwalīna ktatabahā fa-hiya tumlā ʿalayhi bukratan wa-aṣīlan
versets.trad: And they say: “Legends of the ancients which he has had written down, dictated to him morning and evening.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: what they call “legends of the ancients” is only their own nonsense — one does not even know whether such things ever were, and if they were, neither how nor when. They accuse the Qurʾān of being copied, while no age that has passed, no lifetime, could produce a single sūra like it: thus doubt of its truthfulness is dispelled and acknowledgment of its right becomes binding.

mots.racine: س ط ر / أ و ل
mots.def: asāṭīr: tales, writings of the first peoples.

mots.racine: ب ك ر / أ ص ل

versets.ar: قُلْ أَنزَلَهُ الَّذِي يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا
versets.tr: qul anzalahu lladhī yaʿlamu s-sirra fi s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi innahu kāna ghafūran raḥīman
versets.trad: Say: “It was sent down by Him who knows the secret in the heavens and the earth. Truly He is forgiving, compassionate.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī relays the divine reply: O Muḥammad, this Book that He sends down who knows the secret of heavens and earth — none is able to produce its like, even were they all to labor at it together. The enemies of religion, in all their multitude, strove to rival it — ages passed, lifetimes ended, and no one brought a single sūra like it. And the seal of the verse — forgiving, compassionate — still opens for the deniers the door of return.

mots.racine: ع ل م / س ر ر
mots.def: sirr: the hidden, the inmost self.

mots.racine: غ ف ر / ر ح م

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا مَالِ هَٰذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ ۙ لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-qālū mā li-hādha r-rasūli yaʾkulu ṭ-ṭaʿāma wa-yamshī fi l-aswāqi lawlā unzila ilayhi malakun fa-yakūna maʿahu nadhīran
versets.trad: They say: “What sort of messenger is this, who eats food and walks about in the markets? Why has no angel been sent down to him, to be a warner alongside him,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: unable to rival him, they took to faulting him for being a man of their own kind — he walks in the markets, he eats — and reproached him with poverty. They demand a visible angel, heaped-up treasures, signs made to order. But this reproach, while they think it lowers the messenger, only reveals their ignorance of his worth: grace lies not in form but in the hidden meaning.

mots.racine: أ ك ل / ط ع م
م ش ي / س و ق
mots.def: sūq: market, place of ordinary commerce.

versets.ar: أَوْ يُلْقَىٰ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَقَالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا
versets.tr: aw yulqā ilayhi kanzun aw takūnu lahu jannatun yaʾkulu minhā wa-qāla ẓ-ẓālimūna in tattabiʿūna illā rajulan masḥūran
versets.trad: or a treasure been cast down to him, or a garden of his own from which he might eat?” And the wrongdoers say: “You follow none but a bewitched man!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī shows the divine reply to their escalating demands: the Real has power to grant them what they ask and many times more; in His power is to manifest all their fancies and far beyond. But this choice is not theirs to make once a single miracle has lifted every excuse. To demand what one craves is to claim to govern the decree — which does not belong to servants.

mots.racine: ر ج ل / س ح ر

versets.ar: انظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثَالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: unẓur kayfa ḍarabū laka l-amthāla fa-ḍallū fa-lā yastaṭīʿūna sabīlan
versets.trad: See how they coin comparisons for you: they have gone astray and can find no way.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws a doctrinal note from this verse: “they have gone astray and can find no way” proves it is permitted to charge the servant with what he cannot do at the moment, since God declares their incapacity while still holding them responsible and blameworthy. Their comparisons — sorcerer, ordinary man — strike only the one who utters them: it is he who strays and loses the way.

mots.racine: ض ر ب / م ث ل
mots.def: ḍaraba mathalan: to set forth a likeness.

versets.ar: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي إِن شَاءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْرًا مِّن ذَٰلِكَ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ وَيَجْعَل لَّكَ قُصُورًا
versets.tr: tabāraka lladhī in shāʾa jaʿala laka khayran min dhālika jannātin tajrī min taḥtihā l-anhāru wa-yajʿal laka quṣūran
versets.trad: Blessed is He who, if He willed, could grant you better than that: gardens beneath which rivers flow, and palaces too.
versets.commentaire: The verse resumes tabāraka in answer to the mockers: what they demand — treasure, garden — God can give the Prophet multiplied without limit. Qushayrī recalls here the Prophet's sublime refusal: when the keeper of Paradise offered him the keys of the world's treasures, he answered, “I have no need of them; I would rather be a patient, thankful servant.” True wealth is to prefer servitude to possession.

versets.ar: بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالسَّاعَةِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِمَن كَذَّبَ بِالسَّاعَةِ سَعِيرًا
versets.tr: bal kadhdhabū bi-s-sāʿati wa-aʿtadnā li-man kadhdhaba bi-s-sāʿati saʿīran
versets.trad: Rather, they have denied the Hour; and for whoever denies the Hour We have prepared a blaze.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: in God's decree they are counted among the disbelievers, and God has prepared for them, as for their like, the threat of eternity. Inevitably they shall be tried by it. Denial of the Hour is no neutral opinion: it seals a destiny, for to deny the appointed meeting is to deny the One who set it.

versets.ar: إِذَا رَأَتْهُم مِّن مَّكَانٍ بَعِيدٍ سَمِعُوا لَهَا تَغَيُّظًا وَزَفِيرًا
versets.tr: idhā raʾathum min makānin baʿīdin samiʿū lahā taghayyuẓan wa-zafīran
versets.trad: When it sees them from a distant place, they will hear its raging and roaring.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the terror of the Fire is felt from a far distance, before it is seen and before one is tried by it; just as the breeze of the Garden is breathed before one enters it. The Fire has been kindled for years before those who will burn in it, and the Garden adorned for years before those who will enjoy it. Thus is refuted whoever denies the present existence of the two abodes: the Truthful described their attributes, and an attribute is predicated only of an existent.

versets.ar: وَإِذَا أُلْقُوا مِنْهَا مَكَانًا ضَيِّقًا مُّقَرَّنِينَ دَعَوْا هُنَالِكَ ثُبُورًا
versets.tr: wa-idhā ulqū minhā makānan ḍayyiqan muqarranīna daʿaw hunālika thubūran
versets.trad: And when they are flung, bound together, into a narrow place therein, they will cry out for destruction.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī contrasts the two abodes: the rest of the Garden is bound to its breadth, the terror of the Fire to its narrowness. Their place is straitened upon them, their hearts are straitened, their moments are straitened. Could their life cease and could they escape, the trial would not be complete; but they are pains without end, torments that do not conclude.

versets.ar: لَّا تَدْعُوا الْيَوْمَ ثُبُورًا وَاحِدًا وَادْعُوا ثُبُورًا كَثِيرًا
versets.tr: lā tadʿu l-yawma thubūran wāḥidan wa-dʿū thubūran kathīran
versets.trad: “Do not cry out today for a single destruction, but cry out for many destructions!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: whenever they crave a respite, it will be said to them: these are pains that do not conclude, trials that do not end. One death would deliver them, but they are told that no destruction will come to wrest them from torment — “you shall desire nothing but punishment.” The eternity of the Fire is the very absence of the end they call for.

mots.racine: ث ب ر / و ح د

versets.ar: قُلْ أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ الْخُلْدِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ ۚ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزَاءً وَمَصِيرًا
versets.tr: qul a-dhālika khayrun am jannatu l-khuldi llatī wuʿida l-muttaqūna kānat lahum jazāʾan wa-maṣīran
versets.trad: Say: “Is that better, or the Garden of eternity promised to the godfearing, as their reward and their journey's end?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds a praise of the Garden of the godfearing: the godfearing are forever in lasting bliss — maidens and joy, gladness, spirit and fragrance, splendor and beneficence, sweetness ever renewed and favor heaped upon favor, exquisite drink and the cups of the beloved, dilation of the heart and purity of state, the fullness of intimacy, the soul's perpetual rapture. And he warns: the names are the same as in this world, but the realities are wholly other than what is known here below.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / خ ل د
و ع د / و ق ي
mots.def: muttaqī: who fears God, guards himself.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ص ي ر

versets.ar: لَّهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَاءُونَ خَالِدِينَ ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ وَعْدًا مَّسْئُولًا
versets.tr: lahum fīhā mā yashāʾūna khālidīna kāna ʿalā rabbika waʿdan masʾūlan
versets.trad: Therein they shall have whatever they desire, abiding forever. It is a promise binding upon your Lord.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives a subtlety about the will of the blessed: God creates in their hearts only the will for what He knows He will accomplish; what He knows He will not do, their desire does not attach to, and His will keeps it away from their hearts. So their “whatever they desire” coincides perfectly with the decree: they desire only what is granted them, and the accord of willing with the divine Willing is bliss itself.

mots.racine: و ع د / س أ ل
mots.def: masʾūl: whose fulfillment may be asked.

versets.ar: وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ فَيَقُولُ أَأَنتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبَادِي هَٰؤُلَاءِ أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا السَّبِيلَ
versets.tr: wa-yawma yaḥshuruhum wa-mā yaʿbudūna min dūni llāhi fa-yaqūlu a-antum aḍlaltum ʿibādī hāʾulāʾi am hum ḍallu s-sabīla
versets.trad: On the Day He gathers them and what they worshipped besides God, He will say: “Was it you who led astray these My servants, or did they themselves stray from the way?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God gathers the disbelievers and the idols they worshipped; He brings them to life and asks them, “Did you command these to worship you?” And they disown them. All this dialogue is solemn staging, an intimidation to magnify the affair — for He already knows what was and was not. The idols disown their worshippers and give them the lie; and the guilty cry out their own error against themselves.

versets.ar: قَالُوا سُبْحَانَكَ مَا كَانَ يَنبَغِي لَنَا أَن نَّتَّخِذَ مِن دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِيَاءَ وَلَٰكِن مَّتَّعْتَهُمْ وَآبَاءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَسُوا الذِّكْرَ وَكَانُوا قَوْمًا بُورًا
versets.tr: qālū subḥānaka mā kāna yanbaghī lanā an nattakhidha min dūnika min awliyāʾa wa-lākin mattaʿtahum wa-ābāʾahum ḥattā nasu dh-dhikra wa-kānū qawman būran
versets.trad: They will say: “Glory be to You! It was not for us to take protectors besides You; but You let them and their fathers enjoy such ease that they forgot the Reminder, and became a people lost.”
versets.commentaire: The objects of worship glorify God and declare that it was not for them to take protectors besides Him for themselves — how then could they turn others to their own cult? Qushayrī underscores the decisive admission: it was the prolonged enjoyment of bounties, granted to fathers then sons, that made them forget the Reminder — forgetfulness of God is often born of abundance, and comfort becomes ruin (qawm būr) when it makes one lose the memory of the Giver.

mots.racine: ن س ي / ذ ك ر

versets.ar: فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُم بِمَا تَقُولُونَ فَمَا تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفًا وَلَا نَصْرًا ۚ وَمَن يَظْلِم مِّنكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذَابًا كَبِيرًا
versets.tr: fa-qad kadhdhabūkum bi-mā taqūlūna fa-mā tastaṭīʿūna ṣarfan wa-lā naṣran wa-man yaẓlim minkum nudhiqhu ʿadhāban kabīran
versets.trad: [God will say:] “They have given the lie to what you say, so you can find neither escape nor help. And whoever of you does wrong, We shall make him taste a great punishment.”
themes: the lie of the false gods
versets.commentaire: The reversal is complete: those they worshipped become their accusers and give the lie to their words. Qushayrī insists, as in the preceding verses, that the idol disowns and confronts the worshipper with denial, while the worshipper cries out against himself his own straying and error. No more escape (ṣarf) nor help (naṣr): no supposed protector can turn the punishment aside or render aid.

mots.def: denial of the idols toward their worshippers.

mots.racine: ص ر ف / ن ص ر

versets.ar: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ ۗ وَجَعَلْنَا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً أَتَصْبِرُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-mā arsalnā qablaka mina l-mursalīna illā innahum la-yaʾkulūna ṭ-ṭaʿāma wa-yamshūna fi l-aswāqi wa-jaʿalnā baʿḍakum li-baʿḍin fitnatan a-taṣbirūna wa-kāna rabbuka baṣīran
versets.trad: We sent no messengers before you but that they ate food and walked about in the markets. We have made some of you a trial for others: will you be patient? Your Lord is ever seeing.
versets.commentaire: God recalls that all the messengers were men, their sole distinction the miracles: excellences lie in hidden meanings, not in forms. On “We made some of you a trial for others” Qushayrī meditates: He preferred some over others, commanding the lesser-favored to patience and contentment, and the favored to gratitude for the gift. It is neither by their merits nor their deeds, but by His decree, His choice, His favor: by Him and not by them. And “will you be patient?” is a question that carries the force of a command.

mots.def: ṣabara: to endure; question with force of command.

versets.ar: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا ۗ لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-qāla lladhīna lā yarjūna liqāʾanā lawlā unzila ʿalayna l-malāʾikatu aw narā rabbanā la-qadi stakbarū fī anfusihim wa-ʿataw ʿutuwwan kabīran
versets.trad: Those who do not hope for the meeting with Us say: “Why have angels not been sent down upon us, or why do we not see our Lord?” They have grown arrogant within themselves and have overstepped all bounds.
versets.commentaire: “Those who do not hope for the meeting with Us”: they believe neither in the gathering, nor the resurrection, nor the return to God. Qushayrī here opens a delicate distinction: those who demand to see their Lord do so in presumption, thinking they can dictate their terms; but whoever, among the people of the qibla, simply denies the vision (ruʾya) joins these deniers in rejecting what the Tradition has transmitted — for the same Report that announces the gathering announces the vision for the believers.

mots.racine: ر ج و / ل ق ي
mots.sens: do not hope for Our meeting
mots.def: liqāʾ: meeting God on the Last Day.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا
versets.tr: yawma yarawna l-malāʾikata lā bushrā yawmaʾidhin li-l-mujrimīna wa-yaqūlūna ḥijran maḥjūran
versets.trad: On the Day they see the angels — no good tidings that Day for the guilty — they will say: “A barrier forbidden!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they had asked for two things — to see the angels and to see God. He announces that they will indeed see the angels, at the moment of death, but that these will say to them, “No good tidings for you!” And “ḥijran maḥjūran” — forbidden, barred — refers to the vision of God, withheld from them. Therein lies, by implication, the glad tidings for the believers: just as the bliss of the Garden is denied the disbelievers and granted the faithful, so the vision is denied the one and granted the other.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / م ل ك

versets.ar: وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا
versets.tr: wa-qadimnā ilā mā ʿamilū min ʿamalin fa-jaʿalnāhu habāʾan manthūran
versets.trad: And We shall turn to whatever deeds they did, and make them as scattered dust.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws here one of his most beautiful ishārāt. For the disbelievers it is ruin: their effort is lost. But for the people of the realities and the masters of unification, hearing this verse brings to the heart a repose the tongue can scarcely describe: for God said, “We turned to what they did” — and therein is the very worth of their deeds, that He honored them with a glance. The verse goes on: “We made them scattered dust” — and they rejoice, saying: would that we had the works of both worlds, and not one atom of them accepted, so long as concerning them He says, “We turned to…” — for to be freed of the flaws and shames of one's own deeds, they count among the highest of bounties bestowed on them.

mots.def: qadima ilā: to direct oneself toward.

mots.racine: ه ب و / ن ث ر
mots.def: habāʾ: dust in sunbeams; manthūr: dispersed.

versets.ar: أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا
versets.tr: aṣḥābu l-jannati yawmaʾidhin khayrun mustaqarran wa-aḥsanu maqīlan
versets.trad: The companions of the Garden that Day will have the best abode and the fairest place of rest.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the companions of the Garden are those content with it, those who attain it, those for whom its mere presence suffices; hence their dwellings are fair and their abode delightful. The noonday rest (maqīl) evokes quietude at the heart of the Day: for the godfearing, the halt of Judgment is already a place of peace, while for the guilty the same day is torment.

mots.racine: ص ح ب / ج ن ن
mots.def: aṣḥāb: companions, people of the Garden.
maqīl: halt at the hottest of the day.

versets.ar: وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّمَاءُ بِالْغَمَامِ وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-yawma tashaqqaqu s-samāʾu bi-l-ghamāmi wa-nuzzila l-malāʾikatu tanzīlan
versets.trad: The Day the sky is split open with clouds and the angels are sent down in ranks,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: it is the Day of Resurrection, when its terrors appear and its states are manifest to the raised; then they will know, on that Day, with certainty, that the dominion belongs to the All-Merciful. Not that His dominion then became specified: it was ever His — it is their knowledge and certainty that come to them at that moment. The veil of illusions falls, the attributes of creatures vanish, and the Real appears as He is.

versets.ar: الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ ۚ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ عَسِيرًا
versets.tr: al-mulku yawmaʾidhin al-ḥaqqu li-r-raḥmāni wa-kāna yawman ʿala l-kāfirīna ʿasīran
versets.trad: the true dominion that Day belongs to the All-Merciful; and it will be a hard day for the disbelievers,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the motives of all other than Him are cut off, the illusions of creatures vanish; not that a new attribute accrues to Him — exalted is He — but that the attributes of creatures are effaced. And he meditates on the “hard day”: the implied sense is that this day is easy for the believers; otherwise the distinction would be void. There is no believer for whom that day is not light.

mots.racine: م ل ك / ح ق ق

mots.def: ar-Raḥmān: the Name of all-embracing mercy.

versets.ar: وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ الظَّالِمُ عَلَىٰ يَدَيْهِ يَقُولُ يَا لَيْتَنِي اتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ الرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-yawma yaʿaḍḍu ẓ-ẓālimu ʿalā yadayhi yaqūlu yā laytanī ittakhadhtu maʿa r-rasūli sabīlan
versets.trad: the Day the wrongdoer bites his hands, saying: “Would that I had taken a path with the Messenger!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the disbeliever repents of having kept company with the disbelievers; and the implied sense is the joy of the believers at having befriended their companions and beloveds in God. Such is the law of the two fellowships: the disbelieving companion leads his fellow astray and casts him with himself into perdition, while the believing companion guides his fellow to right conduct and brings him with himself to joy. The regret of the Last Day proclaims, by contrast, the worth of spiritual companionship.

mots.racine: ع ض ض / ي د ي
mots.def: ʿaḍḍa: to bite; gesture of extreme regret.

mots.racine: ر س ل / س ب ل
mots.def: to follow the way of the Messenger.

versets.ar: يَا وَيْلَتَىٰ لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا
versets.tr: yā waylatā laytanī lam attakhidh fulānan khalīlan
versets.trad: Woe to me! Would that I had never taken so-and-so as an intimate friend!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums up: the disbeliever regrets the company of disbelievers. Intimate friendship (khulla) binds the soul's eternity. The verse warns against the friend who turns one from the Reminder: the weight of a companionship is measured only on the Day its fruits appear — perdition for whoever followed the misleader, salvation for whoever clung to the guide.

mots.def: ittakhadha: to adopt, to bind oneself.

versets.ar: لَّقَدْ أَضَلَّنِي عَنِ الذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَاءَنِي ۗ وَكَانَ الشَّيْطَانُ لِلْإِنسَانِ خَذُولًا
versets.tr: la-qad aḍallanī ʿani dh-dhikri baʿda idh jāʾanī wa-kāna sh-shayṭānu li-l-insāni khadhūlan
versets.trad: He led me astray from the Reminder after it had come to me. Satan is ever a betrayer of man.”
versets.commentaire: The wrongdoer acknowledges that his friend turned him from the Reminder after it had reached him — the gravest straying is that which comes after the light has arrived. Qushayrī, in keeping with his glosses on companionship, contrasts the friend who misleads with the guide who saves; and Satan (shayṭān), who appears an ally, is revealed at the close as “betrayer” (khadhūl): he abandons at the decisive moment the one he had seduced.

mots.def: aḍalla: to make lose the way.

versets.ar: وَقَالَ الرَّسُولُ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُورًا
versets.tr: wa-qāla r-rasūlu yā rabbi inna qawmi ttakhadhū hādha l-qurʾāna mahjūran
versets.trad: And the Messenger says: “O my Lord, my people have taken this Qurʾān as a thing abandoned!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sets down here a distinction that is one of his pearls: he complained of them to God — and such is the way of the messengers. God reports of Jacob that he said: “I complain of my grief and sorrow only to God.” For whoever complains of God is a renegade, but whoever complains to God is a knower who has found. The whole difference between rebellion and quest lies in that preposition.

versets.ar: وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا مِّنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ هَادِيًا وَنَصِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-kadhālika jaʿalnā li-kulli nabiyyin ʿaduwwan mina l-mujrimīna wa-kafā bi-rabbika hādiyan wa-naṣīran
versets.trad: Thus have We made for every prophet an enemy among the criminals. But your Lord suffices as guide and helper!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: none of His prophets was left without an enemy raised against him in his time. But the verse seals on a consolation of double depth: your Lord suffices you today as guide to His knowledge, and tomorrow as helper for His vision. The hādī leads here below to maʿrifa, the naṣīr guarantees there the ruʾya.

mots.racine: ه د ي / ن ص ر

versets.ar: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً وَاحِدَةً ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤَادَكَ ۖ وَرَتَّلْنَاهُ تَرْتِيلًا
versets.tr: wa-qāla lladhīna kafarū lawlā nuzzila ʿalayhi l-qurʾānu jumlatan wāḥidatan kadhālika li-nuthabbita bihi fuʾādaka wa-rattalnāhu tartīlan
versets.trad: The disbelievers say: “Why was the Qurʾān not sent down upon him all at once?” Thus do We reveal it, to make firm your heart by it, and We have recited it with care.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We sent it down in portions to make its memorization easy for you — for you were unlettered, not reading scriptures. The frequency of its descent was the fitter to calm your heart, perfect your spirit, perpetuate your intimacy: Gabriel came at every moment with what that moment required of unfolding events, and that made the miracle more dazzling, more beyond the suspicion that it came from another. The graded descent weaves the Qurʾān into the very fabric of life.

mots.racine: ج م ل / و ح د
mots.def: in a single block, not spread out.

mots.racine: ث ب ت / ف أ د

versets.ar: وَلَا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ إِلَّا جِئْنَاكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-lā yaʾtūnaka bi-mathalin illā jiʾnāka bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-aḥsana tafsīran
versets.trad: They bring you no comparison but that We bring you the truth and the best interpretation.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the answer to what they advanced as argument was overwhelming, laying bare the falsity of their words. But the Real has established as His way that this adds to the believers only healing and clear-sightedness, and to the deniers only blindness and confusion. The same word heals one and misleads another, according to the disposition of the heart that receives it.

mots.racine: ح س ن / ف س ر

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ أُولَٰئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: alladhīna yuḥsharūna ʿalā wujūhihim ilā jahannama ulāʾika sharrun makānan wa-aḍallu sabīlan
versets.trad: Those who will be gathered upon their faces to Hell — those are worst in station and most astray from the way.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they will be gathered upon their faces, and that is the mark of their abasement. He cites the ḥadīth: “He who makes them walk today upon their feet is able to make them walk tomorrow upon their faces” — and this on His part is in no way impossible. The humiliation of the Last Day is the exact reverse of the arrogance of this world.

mots.def: ḥushira: to be assembled for Judgment.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / س ب ل

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلْنَا مَعَهُ أَخَاهُ هَارُونَ وَزِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-la-qad ātaynā mūsa l-kitāba wa-jaʿalnā maʿahu akhāhu hārūna wazīran
versets.trad: We gave Moses the Book and made his brother Aaron a helper (wazīr) with him.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī observes a law of Qurʾānic rhetoric: rarely does the Qurʾān mention our Prophet without God following the mention with Moses. His story recurs in many places to signal his lofty dignity. And he notes that if singular mention honors the one mentioned, repetition of mention multiplies the detail of praise: a single story, retold, gains in eloquence, especially when it brings each time a fresh meaning.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ك ت ب

mots.def: wazīr: who bears the burden, assists.

versets.ar: فَقُلْنَا اذْهَبَا إِلَى الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا فَدَمَّرْنَاهُمْ تَدْمِيرًا
versets.tr: fa-qulna dhhabā ila l-qawmi lladhīna kadhdhabū bi-āyātinā fa-dammarnāhum tadmīran
versets.trad: And We said: “Go both of you to the people who have denied Our signs.” Then We destroyed them utterly.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes here a stylistic ellipsis: “Go both of you… then We destroyed them” — the verse compresses the beginning and the end, passing over the middle in silence: they went, the people denied, and We destroyed them utterly. And he sees in it a consolation for the Prophet: amid the trials he endured from his people, a promise of beauty — that He will destroy all his enemies.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / أ ي ي

versets.ar: وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ لَّمَّا كَذَّبُوا الرُّسُلَ أَغْرَقْنَاهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ آيَةً ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا
versets.tr: wa-qawma nūḥin lammā kadhdhabu r-rusula aghraqnāhum wa-jaʿalnāhum li-n-nāsi āyatan wa-aʿtadnā li-ẓ-ẓālimīna ʿadhāban alīman
versets.trad: And the people of Noah, when they denied the messengers, We drowned them and made them a sign for mankind; and We have prepared for the wrongdoers a painful punishment.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sets this account within the same consoling purpose: We brought the punishment down upon them, as We did upon their like, dealing with them as We dealt with their fellows. To make the drowned people “a sign for mankind” is to turn ruin into warning: each engulfed generation becomes a letter in the great book of admonition.

mots.def: qawm Nūḥ: the first community punished.

versets.ar: وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَ وَأَصْحَابَ الرَّسِّ وَقُرُونًا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ كَثِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-ʿādan wa-thamūda wa-aṣḥāba r-rassi wa-qurūnan bayna dhālika kathīran
versets.trad: And ʿĀd and Thamūd and the people of al-Rass, and many generations in between,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī passes swiftly over these named peoples (ʿĀd, Thamūd, the people of al-Rass) and the innumerable generations among them, as over those He mentions without detail. All this litany of ruins, he says, aims to calm the Prophet's heart, soothe his inmost self, and let him know that He will destroy whoever fights him and ruin whoever assails him — a promise already kept in his lifetime, and continuing after him.

mots.def: the people of the prophet Hūd.
the people of the prophet Ṣāliḥ.
rass: a well; people of uncertain identity.

versets.ar: وَكُلًّا ضَرَبْنَا لَهُ الْأَمْثَالَ ۖ وَكُلًّا تَبَّرْنَا تَتْبِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-kullan ḍarabnā lahu l-amthāla wa-kullan tabbarnā tatbīran
versets.trad: To each We set forth parables, and each We utterly ruined.
versets.commentaire: To each of these peoples God had set forth parables — clear warnings — before destroying them. Qushayrī, in continuity, reads here the unfolding of justice: warning always precedes sentence, so that ruin falls only after the excuse has been removed. And for the Prophet, it is again the promise that his enemies will not go unpunished.

mots.racine: ض ر ب / م ث ل
mots.sens: We set forth parables for it

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَتَوْا عَلَى الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي أُمْطِرَتْ مَطَرَ السَّوْءِ ۚ أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا يَرَوْنَهَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا لَا يَرْجُونَ نُشُورًا
versets.tr: wa-la-qad ataw ʿala l-qaryati llatī umṭirat maṭara s-sawʾi a-fa-lam yakūnū yarawnahā bal kānū lā yarjūna nushūran
versets.trad: They have passed by the town on which an evil rain was made to fall. Have they not seen it? Rather, they did not hope for any resurrection.
versets.commentaire: This “town” is that of the people of Loṭ, destroyed by a rain of stones for its abominations. Qushayrī links this mention to the series of punishments: all of it to gladden the Prophet's heart, reassure him, and teach him that He will destroy his adversaries. And the root of the Meccans' evil appears: they did not hope for any resurrection — whoever disbelieves in the return draws no lesson from the ruins he passes by.

mots.def: qarya: here the town of Loṭ.

mots.racine: م ط ر / س و أ
mots.def: a rain of stones, a punishment.

mots.racine: ر ج و / ن ش ر

versets.ar: وَإِذَا رَأَوْكَ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَٰذَا الَّذِي بَعَثَ اللَّهُ رَسُولًا
versets.tr: wa-idhā raʾawka in yattakhidhūnaka illā huzuwan a-hādha lladhī baʿatha llāhu rasūlan
versets.trad: When they see you, they take you only in mockery: “Is this the one God has sent as a messenger?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds a fine psychology of consolation: it would have been a comfort to the Prophet to tell his own state and confide his pain; but when it is God who informs him and reports to him what he endures, that comfort is more complete and nearer to intimacy — for the height of consolation for the tried is that their beloveds recount what they suffered in the days of trial. That God Himself should tell the Prophet of his pain is the supreme solace.

mots.racine: ب ع ث / ر س ل
mots.sens: God has sent as a messenger

versets.ar: إِن كَادَ لَيُضِلُّنَا عَنْ آلِهَتِنَا لَوْلَا أَن صَبَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ حِينَ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذَابَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: in kāda la-yuḍillunā ʿan ālihatinā lawlā an ṣabarnā ʿalayhā wa-sawfa yaʿlamūna ḥīna yarawna l-ʿadhāba man aḍallu sabīlan
versets.trad: He almost led us away from our gods, had we not stood firm by them.” But they will know, when they see the punishment, who is most astray from the way.
versets.commentaire: The deniers congratulate themselves on their “steadfastness” in clinging to their idols, mistaking obstinacy for virtue. The verse turns against them the term furqān — discernment — that gives the sūra its name: they think they discern and choose, but it is their passion that holds them. They will know, when they see the punishment, who was most astray: true steadfastness would have been to detach from the idol, not to cling to it.

mots.racine: ك و د / ض ل ل
mots.def: kāda: to be on the point of.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / س ب ل

versets.ar: أَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَٰهَهُ هَوَاهُ أَفَأَنتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلًا
versets.tr: a-raʾayta mani ttakhadha ilāhahu hawāhu a-fa-anta takūnu ʿalayhi wakīlan
versets.trad: Have you seen the one who takes his own passion as his god? Would you then be a guardian over him?
versets.commentaire: Here is one of the key verses of inner discernment. Qushayrī: they worshipped among the idols whatever their whim dictated, exchanging one idol for another, following what crossed their souls. But the believer acts by the decree of God, not by the decree of his own soul — and by this the furqān is manifest, the dividing between one man and another. Whoever lives by what pleases him worships his passion, and joins those this verse blames.

mots.racine: أ ل ه / ه و ي
mots.sens: he took his passion as his god
mots.def: hawā: whim, desire of the soul.

versets.ar: أَمْ تَحْسَبُ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ أَوْ يَعْقِلُونَ ۚ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَالْأَنْعَامِ ۖ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: am taḥsabu anna aktharahum yasmaʿūna aw yaʿqilūna in hum illā ka-l-anʿāmi bal hum aḍallu sabīlan
versets.trad: Or do you think that most of them hear or reason? They are but like cattle — nay, further astray from the way.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī delivers here one of his great maxims: they are like cattle that have no care but eating and drinking; whoever pursues the portions of his soul is like the beasts. Now God created the angels upon the warp of intellect, the beasts upon the warp of passion, and composed both in man: he in whom passion conquers intellect is worse than the beasts, and he in whom intellect conquers passion is better than the angels — thus spoke the masters.

mots.racine: س م ع / ع ق ل
mots.def: samiʿa: to listen; ʿaqala: to understand.

versets.ar: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شَاءَ لَجَعَلَهُ سَاكِنًا ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلًا
versets.tr: a-lam tara ilā rabbika kayfa madda ẓ-ẓilla wa-law shāʾa la-jaʿalahu sākinan thumma jaʿalna sh-shamsa ʿalayhi dalīlan
versets.trad: Have you not seen how your Lord extends the shadow? Had He willed, He would have made it still. Then We made the sun its guide,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds here one of his loftiest contemplations: have you not seen how your Lord has extended the shadow of solicitude over the states of His beloveds? Some are in the shadow of protection, others in the shadow of guarding, others in the shadow of grace; the poor in the shadow of sufficiency, the rich in the shadow of repose. There is a shadow of preservation for the prophets and the saints, and a shadow of mercy for the believers, then for all creatures. And he adds a vertiginous subtlety: God said to Moses, “You shall not see Me,” but to our Prophet, “Have you not seen your Lord?” — what a distance between the two! He made him live by “Have you not seen your Lord,” then effaced him by “how He extended the shadow”: such is His way with His servants, making them alternate between extinction and subsistence.

mots.racine: م د د / ظ ل ل
mots.def: ẓill: shadow; madda: to spread out.

mots.racine: ش م س / د ل ل

versets.ar: ثُمَّ قَبَضْنَاهُ إِلَيْنَا قَبْضًا يَسِيرًا
versets.tr: thumma qabaḍnāhu ilaynā qabḍan yasīran
versets.trad: then We draw it in toward Us, an easy drawing.
versets.commentaire: The shadow, spread at dawn, is gently drawn in as the sun rises — “an easy drawing.” Qushayrī makes of this ebb and flow the image of divine pedagogy: He made him live by “Have you not seen your Lord,” then effaced him by “how He extended the shadow”; such is His way with His servants: He makes them oscillate ceaselessly between extinction (fanāʾ) and subsistence (baqāʾ). The shadow's withdrawal toward Him figures the return of all things to their source.

mots.sens: We drew it in, withdrew it

mots.racine: ق ب ض / ي س ر

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا وَالنَّوْمَ سُبَاتًا وَجَعَلَ النَّهَارَ نُشُورًا
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī jaʿala lakumu l-layla libāsan wa-n-nawma subātan wa-jaʿala n-nahāra nushūran
versets.trad: It is He who made the night for you a garment, sleep for repose, and made the day a resurrection.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives here one of his tenderest pages: He made the night a time of rest for some, a time of turmoil for others: the heedless sleep in their night, but the lovers keep vigil in theirs. If they are in the joy of union, sleep does not take them, so perfect is their intimacy; if they are in the pain of separation, sleep flees them, so great is their disquiet. The vigil of the beloved is a sign — either of the height of joy or of the onslaught of cares. And he adds: sleep is a punishment for the heedless, but a mercy for the strivers, whom the Real puts to sleep out of compassion for their souls, that they may rest from the toil of spiritual struggle.

mots.racine: ل ي ل / ل ب س
mots.def: libās: that which covers and envelops.

mots.racine: ن و م / س ب ت
ن ه ر / ن ش ر

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّيَاحَ بُشْرًا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً طَهُورًا
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī arsala r-riyāḥa bushran bayna yaday raḥmatihi wa-anzalnā mina s-samāʾi māʾan ṭahūran
versets.trad: It is He who sends the winds as bearers of good tidings before His mercy. And We send down from the sky pure water,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī transposes the winds into inner breaths: He sends the winds of generosity, which blow upon the hearts of the needy and drive them to seek His bounties; the winds of sanctity, which blow upon the hearts of the elite and purify them of every self-will, until they suffice themselves with God for God; the winds of fear upon the hearts of the rebellious, bearing them to remorse and return; the winds of yearning upon the hearts of the beloved, tearing them from all repose and letting them find rest only in unveiling. And he seals: when the heart breathes the breeze of nearness, it wanders, lost, in the kingdom of Majesty, effaced of every trace and landmark.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ر و ح
م و ه / ط ه ر

versets.ar: لِّنُحْيِيَ بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا وَنُسْقِيَهُ مِمَّا خَلَقْنَا أَنْعَامًا وَأَنَاسِيَّ كَثِيرًا
versets.tr: li-nuḥyiya bihi baldatan maytan wa-nusqiyahu mimmā khalaqnā anʿāman wa-anāsiyya kathīran
versets.trad: to revive thereby a dead land and to give drink to the many beasts and humans We have created.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī doubles the material water with a spiritual one: He sent down the water of rain that revives meadows and orchards, and the water of mercy that washes the rebellious of their stains. The word ṭahūr means the pure that purifies: the water of modesty purifies the hearts of the knowers of every inclination to deceptive repose; the water of solicitude revives the hearts of the yearning with the lights of unveiling, until it quenches the thirst of longing; and it revives souls dead from the pursuit of passions, restoring them to the fulfillment of worship.

mots.racine: ب ل د / م و ت
ن ع م / أ ن س

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَاهُ بَيْنَهُمْ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا فَأَبَىٰ أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا
versets.tr: wa-la-qad ṣarrafnāhu baynahum li-yadhdhakkarū fa-abā aktharu n-nāsi illā kufūran
versets.trad: We have distributed it among them that they might remember; but most people refuse all but ingratitude.
versets.commentaire: The gift of water, apportioned and varied, is destined for remembrance (dhikr); but most retain from it only ingratitude. Qushayrī, having developed the water of mercy and of modesty, here underscores the contrast: grace descends to awaken remembrance, and the heedless soul answers only with denial. The same bounty stirs gratitude (ḥamd) in one and ingratitude (kufr) in another, according to the slope of the heart.

versets.ar: وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَبَعَثْنَا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ نَّذِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-law shiʾnā la-baʿathnā fī kulli qaryatin nadhīran
versets.trad: Had We willed, We would have raised a warner in every town.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sees in this verse an honor done to the Prophet: God distinguished our Prophet by preferring him to all, by sending him to the whole of mankind, and by making his Law forever unabrogated. By this verse He educates him with the most subtle of allusions. He relates the account in which, when Moses one day wearied of the petitions, God raised in a single night a thousand prophets among the children of Israel, and the crowd left Moses — who was grieved; then God took back their souls that very day. The Real's design is that His elect remain forever preserved in their stations.

versets.ar: فَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَجَاهِدْهُم بِهِ جِهَادًا كَبِيرًا
versets.tr: fa-lā tuṭiʿi l-kāfirīna wa-jāhidhum bihi jihādan kabīran
versets.trad: So do not obey the disbelievers, but strive against them with it (the Qurʾān) in a mighty striving.
themes: the great striving by the Qurʾān
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads this verse as a call to inner rectitude: stand for Our right with no least inclination toward another than Us, no least concern for any but Us; for We protect you from every side, and We never lift from you the shadow of Our solicitude. The “great jihād” by the Qurʾān is first of all this total fidelity that does not deviate toward the other than God.

mots.racine: ج ه د / ك ب ر

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ هَٰذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ وَهَٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī maraja l-baḥrayni hādhā ʿadhbun furātun wa-hādhā milḥun ujājun wa-jaʿala baynahumā barzakhan wa-ḥijran maḥjūran
versets.trad: It is He who let flow the two seas together — one sweet and refreshing, the other salt and bitter — and set between them a barrier, an insurmountable partition.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of the two seas a parable of hearts: the salt sea has no sweetness, the sweet no saltness; yet they are one water in their substance — it is He who, by His power, differentiated them in attribute. Thus He created the hearts: some a mine of certainty and knowledge, others a vessel of doubt and denial. And he adds a subtlety on spiritual balance: He established in the hearts of the believers fear and hope, with neither fear overcoming hope, nor hope overcoming fear.

mots.racine: م ر ج / ب ح ر
mots.sens: He let the two seas flow

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ف ر ت
م ل ح / أ ج ج

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْمَاءِ بَشَرًا فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī khalaqa mina l-māʾi basharan fa-jaʿalahu nasaban wa-ṣihran wa-kāna rabbuka qadīran
versets.trad: It is He who created from water a human being, then made of him kinship of blood and of marriage. Your Lord is ever powerful.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī meditates on the unity of origin and the diversity of destinies: creatures resemble one another in the principle of their creation, identical in substance, yet divergent in attribute and different in form. The souls of the enemies are their mounts that drive them to the Fire, the souls of the believers their mounts that bear them to the Garden. All are human… but not every human is like such a human: one is an enemy, who lives only by his portion and his lot; the other is a saint, who never slackens his obedience nor descends from his lofty aspiration.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / م و ه
mots.sens: He created from water a human

versets.ar: وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُهُمْ وَلَا يَضُرُّهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ الْكَافِرُ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِ ظَهِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-yaʿbudūna min dūni llāhi mā lā yanfaʿuhum wa-lā yaḍurruhum wa-kāna l-kāfiru ʿalā rabbihi ẓahīran
versets.trad: They worship besides God what neither benefits nor harms them. The disbeliever is ever a helper against his Lord.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī contrasts the idol-worshipper with the free believer: he contents himself with what is carved from wood, fashioned from stone, cast from copper — inert things that neither reason nor hear, neither harm nor benefit. The believer, by contrast, has as his sign that he does not even turn his gaze toward the Throne — sublime though it be — nor bend his heart before any creature, though it be adorned with countless merits. The heart's freedom is to bow only before the One.

mots.def: ẓahīr: who lends aid (here to God's enemies).

versets.ar: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-mā arsalnāka illā mubashshiran wa-nadhīran
versets.trad: We have sent you only as a bearer of good tidings and a warner.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: you are a messenger from Us, charged with warning and announcing, standing where We have placed you in the function of conveying, asking no wage of them, coveting no portion of them. The mission resolves into two movements — the joy promised and the warning given — with no self-interest.

versets.ar: قُلْ مَا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَن شَاءَ أَن يَتَّخِذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا
versets.tr: qul mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin illā man shāʾa an yattakhidha ilā rabbihi sabīlan
versets.trad: Say: “I ask of you no wage for it, except that whoever wishes may take a path to his Lord.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī clarifies the grammatical subtlety: the “except” (illā) introduces a detached exception, for the seeking of a way to their Lord is not a wage he would take from them. The only “gain” the Prophet desires is their own journeying toward God — he is a herald for whoever turns to Him, a warner for whoever turns away.

mots.racine: س أ ل / أ ج ر
mots.sens: I ask of you no wage

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / س ب ل

versets.ar: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لَا يَمُوتُ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِ خَبِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-tawakkal ʿala l-ḥayyi lladhī lā yamūtu wa-sabbiḥ bi-ḥamdihi wa-kafā bihi bi-dhunūbi ʿibādihi khabīran
versets.trad: Put your trust in the Living who does not die, and glorify Him with praise. He suffices as knower of the sins of His servants.
versets.commentaire: This verse gives Qushayrī occasion for a great exposition of tawakkul: trusting reliance is to commit one's affairs to God; its foundation is the servant's knowledge that all events proceed from God, and that none but He can bring them into being. Once this is grasped, the heart is at rest. Then he climbs its degrees: contentment (qanāʿa); then quietude of heart when causes fail; tawakkul as “the heart's rest in the Lord's guarantee”; above it total surrender (taslīm); then self-relinquishment (tafwīḍ) — committing one's affair to God without suggesting anything to Him, without choosing, the presence and absence of causes becoming equal; then contentment (riḍā), where one finds rest even in refusal; then conformity, where, at the breeze of nearness, one forgets every desire; finally the seizure of the Reality, where the servant is wholly taken — extinction, annihilation, effacement: this is the very essence of unification.

mots.racine: ح ي ي / م و ت
mots.sens: the Living who does not die

mots.def: khabīr: who knows the depths of things.

versets.ar: الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ ۚ الرَّحْمَٰنُ فَاسْأَلْ بِهِ خَبِيرًا
versets.tr: alladhī khalaqa s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍa wa-mā baynahumā fī sittati ayyāmin thumma stawā ʿala l-ʿarshi r-raḥmānu fa-sʾal bihi khabīran
versets.trad: He who created the heavens and the earth and all between them in six days, then established Himself upon the Throne — the All-Merciful. Ask, then, about Him one well-informed.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sets aside all anthropomorphic reading: the universe — and the Throne is part of the universe — was ordered by Him; the Real adorned Himself with none of His creatures to manifest them. His elevation above the Throne is by His dominance and power, and His istiwāʾ is an act by which He singled out the Throne, perfecting its parts and its form. Majesty does not stoop to inhabit a place: it ordains and embraces.

mots.racine: س د س / ي و م
س و ي / ع ر ش
mots.sens: He established Himself on the Throne
mots.def: istiwāʾ: understood as dominion, not place.

versets.ar: وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اسْجُدُوا لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ قَالُوا وَمَا الرَّحْمَٰنُ أَنَسْجُدُ لِمَا تَأْمُرُنَا وَزَادَهُمْ نُفُورًا
versets.tr: wa-idhā qīla lahumu sjudū li-r-raḥmāni qālū wa-ma r-raḥmānu a-nasjudu li-mā taʾmurunā wa-zādahum nufūran
versets.trad: When it is said to them, “Prostrate to the All-Merciful,” they say, “And what is the All-Merciful? Shall we prostrate to whatever you command us?” And it only increases their aversion.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī attributes this refusal to the precedence of the decree: the Real turned by His gentleness and favor toward certain peoples, and they found Him; He turned away from others by His grandeur and pride, and they denied Him. He fashioned them upon the mark of distance, kneaded their clay with the water of wretchedness and rejection; so when He brought them forth, He clothed them in the garment of ignorance and denial. The “what is the All-Merciful?” betrays a sealed heart.

mots.racine: س ج د / ر ح م
ز ي د / ن ف ر

versets.ar: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ فِي السَّمَاءِ بُرُوجًا وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا سِرَاجًا وَقَمَرًا مُّنِيرًا
versets.tr: tabāraka lladhī jaʿala fi s-samāʾi burūjan wa-jaʿala fīhā sirājan wa-qamaran munīran
versets.trad: Blessed is He who placed constellations in the sky and placed in it a lamp and a shining moon.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī doubles the visible sky with an inner one, in one of his most beautiful pages: just as He established constellations in the sky, He established in the sky of the hearts of His saints constellations: those of the sky are numbered, those of the heart are contemplated. The stars of the heart are intelligence, understanding, clear-sightedness, knowledge; the moon of hearts is gnosis (maʿrifa). And he rises higher still: the moon of the sky wanes and is eclipsed, but the moon of gnosis ever has its radiance without decline; the sun of hearts is unification (tawḥīd) — the sun of day sets, but the sun of hearts neither sets nor disappears.

mots.def: burūj: towers, signs of the zodiac.

mots.racine: ق م ر / ن و ر

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهَارَ خِلْفَةً لِّمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرَادَ شُكُورًا
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī jaʿala l-layla wa-n-nahāra khilfatan li-man arāda an yadhdhakkara aw arāda shukūran
versets.trad: It is He who made the night and the day a succession for whoever wishes to remember or wishes to give thanks.
themes: the succession of night and day
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of the alternation of night and day a mercy for the worshipper: the times are alike in themselves; one is preferred to another only in that obedience is better in it and the reward greater. The night is the lining of the day, the day the lining of the night: whoever has missed a devotion of the night repairs it when day comes, and whoever has fallen short by day completes it at night. Time ceaselessly offers the heart a second chance — khilfa, the reparative relay.

mots.def: khilfa: that which succeeds and supplies.

versets.ar: وَعِبَادُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا وَإِذَا خَاطَبَهُمُ الْجَاهِلُونَ قَالُوا سَلَامًا
versets.tr: wa-ʿibādu r-raḥmāni lladhīna yamshūna ʿala l-arḍi hawnan wa-idhā khāṭabahumu l-jāhilūna qālū salāman
versets.trad: The servants of the All-Merciful are those who walk upon the earth in humility, and who, when the ignorant address them, say: “Peace (Salām)!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī here opens the great portrait of the ʿibād al-Raḥmān: those who have merited the mercy of the All-Merciful are those granted the success of obedience; it is by His mercy that they reached this success — such is the speech of the Reality; and by their obedience that they reached His Garden — such is the tongue of the Law. “Hawnan” means humble, self-effacing. And on their reply to the ignorant: whoever addresses them with blame, they repay with praise; to coarseness they answer with gentleness, fair character, and fair speech.

mots.racine: ع ب د / ر ح م
mots.def: title of the elect of the Raḥmān.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَامًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna yabītūna li-rabbihim sujjadan wa-qiyāman
versets.trad: and those who pass the night before their Lord, prostrate and standing,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they pass the night for their Lord, prostrate, and rise at morning having found: the finding of their morning is the fruit of the prostration of their spirits. And he relates the ḥadīth: “Whoever multiplies his prayer at night, his face is made beautiful by day” — that is, his face gains radiance before God: the fairest of things is an outward beautified by prostration and an inward adorned by finding.

mots.sens: they pass the night for their Lord

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذَابَ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ إِنَّ عَذَابَهَا كَانَ غَرَامًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna yaqūlūna rabbana ṣrif ʿannā ʿadhāba jahannama inna ʿadhābahā kāna gharāman
versets.trad: and those who say: “Our Lord, turn away from us the punishment of Hell, for its punishment is a relentless torment.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī admires the paradoxical humility of these servants: they strive to the utmost of effort, exhaust all their capacity, and yet, at the moment of asking, they lower themselves to the rank of sinners, stand in the station of those who plead for excuse, and address God in the tongue of repentance. The more they strive, the smaller they make themselves — their very devotion inspires in them no pride, but the posture of the humbled servant at the threshold.

mots.def: gharām: persistent attachment, a scourge that does not let go.

versets.ar: إِنَّهَا سَاءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا
versets.tr: innahā sāʾat mustaqarran wa-muqāman
versets.trad: Truly, evil is it as an abode and a dwelling.”
versets.commentaire: The prayer of the servants of the All-Merciful continues: Hell is a wretched place of halt and of dwelling. Qushayrī, faithful to his reading of their humility, sees in this admission the mark of those who, despite their intense devotion, never rest upon their works but dread the baleful abode — their fear is proportioned to their knowledge of the gravity of the punishment.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ إِذَا أَنفَقُوا لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ قَوَامًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna idhā anfaqū lam yusrifū wa-lam yaqturū wa-kāna bayna dhālika qawāman
versets.trad: and those who, when they spend, are neither prodigal nor miserly, but hold a balance between the two,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī spiritualizes the two excesses: prodigality (isrāf) is to spend on passion and the soul's portion — but what is spent for God knows no prodigality; and stinginess (iqtār) is to withhold what is due to God. As for restricting oneself, to wean the soul from following appetites and accustom it to be content with little, that is not the blamed miserliness. The just measure (qawām) is judged by intention, not by the sum.

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ لَا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلَٰهًا آخَرَ وَلَا يَقْتُلُونَ النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَا يَزْنُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثَامًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna lā yadʿūna maʿa llāhi ilāhan ākhara wa-lā yaqtulūna n-nafsa llatī ḥarrama llāhu illā bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-lā yaznūna wa-man yafʿal dhālika yalqa athāman
versets.trad: and those who do not call upon another god alongside God, nor kill the soul God has forbidden except by right, nor commit fornication — and whoever does that shall meet the wage of sin.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī interiorizes each prohibition: “another god” is in appearance the idol of stone or wood; but likewise, to ascribe good and harm to another than Him is a form of associationism. “Do not kill the soul” also aims at the servant's own poor soul: to kill it unjustly is to deliver it to the pursuit of what destroys it in the other world. And he admirably turns the exception “except by right”: so kill it “by the right” — that is, slaughter it with the knife of spiritual struggles (mujāhadāt); for your salvation is only in the killing of the soul that lies between your sides.

mots.racine: د ع و / أ ل ه
mots.sens: they call upon no other god

mots.racine: ق ت ل / ن ف س
mots.sens: they do not kill the soul

mots.def: legitimate exception; read by Qushayrī as the mujāhada.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / أ ث م
mots.sens: he meets the wage of sin

versets.ar: يُضَاعَفْ لَهُ الْعَذَابُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهَانًا
versets.tr: yuḍāʿaf lahu l-ʿadhābu yawma l-qiyāmati wa-yakhlud fīhi muhānan
versets.trad: His punishment will be doubled on the Day of Resurrection, and he will abide therein, abased, forever.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī adds a present dimension to the future punishment: their punishment will be doubled on the Day of Resurrection by the sorrows of separation and the gasps of burning. But others see their punishment doubled already here below, by the accumulation of abandonment, the haste of rupture, the permanence of deprivation; rather, he whose punishment is double in the hereafter is the one whose punishment was double in this world. And he cites the ḥadīth: “One dies in the state in which one will meet God.”

mots.sens: the punishment will be doubled for him

versets.ar: إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا فَأُولَٰئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللَّهُ سَيِّئَاتِهِمْ حَسَنَاتٍ ۗ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا
versets.tr: illā man tāba wa-āmana wa-ʿamila ʿamalan ṣāliḥan fa-ulāʾika yubaddilu llāhu sayyiʾātihim ḥasanātin wa-kāna llāhu ghafūran raḥīman
versets.trad: Except those who repent, believe, and do righteous deeds: for those God will change their evil deeds into good ones. And God is ever forgiving, compassionate.
versets.commentaire: Here is one of the verses of mercy where Qushayrī unfolds hope: “whoever repented” of sin in the moment, “and believed” in the outcome; one also says: his salvation is by God's grace, not by his repentance. And on the transmutation: God changes their sins into good deeds; He creates for them success in place of abandonment; He effaces the lowliness of their slips and inscribes instead good things and fair deeds. The largesse of divine mercy is too vast to narrow before one who has stumbled.

mots.racine: ع م ل / ص ل ح
ب د ل / ح س ن
mots.sens: changes their evils into good deeds

versets.ar: وَمَن تَابَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَإِنَّهُ يَتُوبُ إِلَى اللَّهِ مَتَابًا
versets.tr: wa-man tāba wa-ʿamila ṣāliḥan fa-innahu yatūbu ila llāhi matāban
versets.trad: And whoever repents and does righteous deeds turns to God with a true returning.
versets.commentaire: Repentance is fully a return only if followed by upright deeds. Qushayrī, in continuity with his gloss on tawba, sees in the “matāb” — the true return — the seal of authentic repentance: not a passing remorse, but a conversion that reorients the whole of life toward God. And it was said: if the penitent breaks his repentance, let him then act with uprightness — that is, let him renew his repentance.

mots.racine: ع م ل / ص ل ح

mots.sens: he returns with a true returning

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ لَا يَشْهَدُونَ الزُّورَ وَإِذَا مَرُّوا بِاللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا كِرَامًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna lā yashhadūna z-zūra wa-idhā marrū bi-l-laghwi marrū kirāman
versets.trad: And those who bear no false witness, and who, when they pass by frivolity, pass by with dignity,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they stand firm at the dwellings of truthfulness, night and day, in word and deed. When they pass by the people of slips and the abodes of transgression, they pass as men made firm, turned away, without lingering with their occupants. And he relates that the verse came down concerning those who, having entered Mecca, passed by the houses where idols had once been worshipped with nobility, without even casting a glance — and God was grateful to them for it.

mots.racine: ش ه د / ز و ر
م ر ر / ل غ و

mots.sens: they pass by as noble ones

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَمْ يَخِرُّوا عَلَيْهَا صُمًّا وَعُمْيَانًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna idhā dhukkirū bi-āyāti rabbihim lam yakhirrū ʿalayhā ṣumman wa-ʿumyānan
versets.trad: and those who, when reminded of the signs of their Lord, do not fall upon them deaf and blind,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when they are reminded of the signs of their Lord, they do not throw themselves upon them deaf and blind, but receive them with reflection, meditation, and the exercise of the gaze. The contrast is with the mechanical devotee: to hear the verses without the heart awakening is to fall upon them “deaf and blind.” The true servant receives each sign as a call to living contemplation.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / أ ي ي

mots.sens: they do not fall upon them

mots.racine: ص م م / ع م ي

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا هَبْ لَنَا مِنْ أَزْوَاجِنَا وَذُرِّيَّاتِنَا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ وَاجْعَلْنَا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِمَامًا
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna yaqūlūna rabbanā hab lanā min azwājinā wa-dhurriyyātinā qurrata aʿyunin wa-jʿalnā li-l-muttaqīna imāman
versets.trad: and those who say: “Our Lord, grant us from our spouses and our offspring the coolness of eyes, and make us an example for the godfearing,”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī spiritualizes the “coolness of eyes”: the coolness of eyes is the one in whom the life of the spirit dwells; and he is such only on condition of fulfilling the right of God. The coolness of eyes is the one who embraces the obedience of his Lord and parts from the transgression of His command. And on leadership: the imam is the one who is imitated and does not innovate; God praised men who mentioned the rank of imam and asked for it in humility, without claiming they had chosen it — for the imamate is attained by supplication (duʿāʾ), not by claim (daʿwā).

mots.racine: ق ر ر / ع ي ن
و ق ي / أ م م

versets.ar: أُولَٰئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ الْغُرْفَةَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا وَيُلَقَّوْنَ فِيهَا تَحِيَّةً وَسَلَامًا
versets.tr: ulāʾika yujzawna l-ghurfata bi-mā ṣabarū wa-yulaqqawna fīhā taḥiyyatan wa-salāman
versets.trad: those will be rewarded with the highest chambers for their patience, and there they will be met with greeting and peace,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He gives much of His giving and counts it little; He accepts the little of the servant's obedience and holds it great and immense. And he discloses the marvel of “they will be met with greeting and peace”: they will hear His greeting upon them without intermediary, and He will unveil Himself to them that they may see Him — without transport or traversing of distance. On “for their patience”: they patiently kept from what was forbidden them, patiently bore the decrees He ran upon them, abandoning their own choice in the fair contentment of His measure.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / غ ر ف
mots.sens: they will be rewarded with the chamber

mots.racine: ح ي ي / س ل م

versets.ar: خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا
versets.tr: khālidīna fīhā ḥasunat mustaqarran wa-muqāman
versets.trad: abiding therein forever — how excellent an abode and a dwelling!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: abiding forever, never leaving their dwellings; and in their states, fair is their abode as an abode, fair their dwelling as a dwelling. In perfect mirror of the “evil dwelling” of Hell (v.66), the Garden is called “an excellent abode and dwelling”: the same pair of words distinguishes the end of the two ways, the outcome of the ʿibād al-Raḥmān and that of the deniers.

mots.def: ḥasuna: to be excellent (verb of praise).

mots.racine: ق ر ر / ق و م

versets.ar: قُلْ مَا يَعْبَأُ بِكُمْ رَبِّي لَوْلَا دُعَاؤُكُمْ ۖ فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًا
versets.tr: qul mā yaʿbaʾu bikum rabbī lawlā duʿāʾukum fa-qad kadhdhabtum fa-sawfa yakūnu lizāman
versets.trad: Say: “My Lord would not care for you were it not for your supplication. But you have given the lie, so it will be an inescapable consequence.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī seals the sūra with two readings of supplication: were it not for your worship of the idols and your claim of naming them gods worthy of worship, why would He make you abide eternally in the Fire? And a luminous reading for the believers: were it not for your humble supplication and your invocation full of imploring fervor, He would have made the trial endure upon you; but when you abased yourselves in humility and recourse, when you implored Him, He had mercy on you and lifted the affliction from you. Thus the duʿāʾ — the servant's call — gives man all his worth before God.

mots.sens: He would not care for you
mots.def: ʿabaʾa: to heed, to give weight to.

mots.sens: were it not for your supplication
it will be an inescapable consequence
